args: A 61-year-old man with a remote history of total laryngectomy for T3 squamous-cell carcinoma of the larynx, chronic atrial fibrillation on rate control (no anticoagulant by patient preference), and insulin-dependent type 2 diabetes was brought to the emergency department by his home health nurse after she found him on the bedroom floor next to his powered wheelchair roughly thirty minutes before arrival; according to her report he was gesturing for help but produced no sound and appeared dyspneic, so emergency medical services were summoned. Paramedics state that on scene he was breathing spontaneously through his permanent tracheostomy stoma, had a portable electrolarynx lying unused on the nightstand, displayed no signs of seizure activity, and had a capillary glucose of 108 mg/dL; no medications were administered en route apart from 2 L/min supplemental oxygen through a tracheostomy collar. On entry to the resuscitation bay the patient is supine, eyes already open and tracking personnel purposefully as the stretcher is positioned, blinking normally and shifting gaze between speakers without any external prompting. Airway inspection reveals a mature 1.5 cm tracheostoma with a cuffless, nonfenestrated tube in place; humidification collar delivering 28 % FiO₂ is connected and misting appropriately. He is unable to phonate by design of his altered anatomy, and no guttural sounds, moans, or whispers are produced even when repeatedly asked to state his name; instead he mouths words silently, nods or shakes his head, and writes short phrases on a clipboard handed to him. Vital signs obtained prior to any emergency department intervention are temperature 37.2 °C orally, heart rate 96 beats/min and irregularly irregular, blood pressure 138/78 mm Hg in the right brachial artery, respiratory rate 22 breaths/min with modest accessory muscle use, and oxygen saturation 94 % on the aforementioned trach collar. Pupils measure 3 mm bilaterally and are briskly reactive to light; extraocular movements are intact and conjugate without nystagmus. Facial symmetry is preserved and corneal reflexes present. Cranial nerve testing otherwise demonstrates intact eyebrow raise, eyelid closure, and shoulder shrug on request. When instructed—using gestures and written prompts—the patient purposefully squeezes the examiner’s fingers, releases upon command, lifts each arm to 90 degrees, wiggles fingers, flexes hips against gravity, and plantar-flexes as directed, all without delay or drift; no abnormal posturing, spasticity, or flaccidity is noted. Sensory examination shows intact light-touch appreciation in all extremities; rapid alternating movements are slightly slowed on the right, consistent with prior baseline according to the nurse. Deep tendon reflexes are 2+ and symmetric, and plantar responses are downgoing. He blinks twice in response to “blink twice if you can feel this” when the examiner lightly pinches the extensor surface of his left forearm, confirming comprehension. Skin survey reveals a well-healed midline neck scar, no fresh trauma, and warm, well-perfused extremities with capillary refill under two seconds. Cardiac auscultation detects an irregularly irregular rhythm without murmurs, rubs, or gallops; lung fields are clear to auscultation bilaterally with coarse air movement near the stoma but no wheezes or crackles. Abdomen is soft, nontender, and nondistended; bowel sounds are present. Peripheral pulses are 2+ in all limbs, and there is no lower-extremity edema. Point-of-care arterial blood gas sampled on the trach collar prior to any manipulation shows pH 7.37, PaCO₂ 43 mm Hg, PaO₂ 76 mm Hg, bicarbonate 24 mEq/L, and base excess 0 mEq/L. Bedside chemistry panel reveals sodium 138 mEq/L, potassium 4.0 mEq/L, chloride 101 mEq/L, bicarbonate 25 mEq/L, blood urea nitrogen 20 mg/dL, creatinine 1.1 mg/dL, and glucose 106 mg/dL. Hematocrit measures 39 %, white blood cell count 7.8 × 10⁹/L, and platelet count 238 × 10⁹/L. Coagulation profile shows INR 1.1 and aPTT 32 seconds. No sedative, paralytic, or analgesic agents have been administered prior to this assessment, and he denies (by head shake) any ingestion of alcohol, opioids, or illicit substances today. Home medications include metoprolol tartrate 25 mg twice daily, insulin glargine 22 units nightly with sliding scale lispro, and atorvastatin 20 mg nightly; the visiting nurse confirms adherence. Last insulin dose was the previous evening. Family history is notable only for coronary artery disease in a sibling. The patient lives alone, communicates normally with an electrolarynx, and is fully independent in activities of daily living; no previous cerebrovascular events are documented. At this point his mental status appears intact, he maintains eye contact without external stimulus, follows every written or gestured instruction accurately, yet remains completely silent due to anatomical absence of the vocal apparatus.
What is the patient's Glasgow Coma Score? You should use the patient's medical values and health status when they were first admitted to the hospital prior to any treatment.

step_info.args:
{"focus": "Glasgow Coma Scale (GCS) for a 61-year-old male with total laryngectomy:
- Eye opening: Patient's eyes are already open, tracking personnel purposefully, shifting gaze, and blinking normally (E4).
- Verbal response: Patient has a total laryngectomy and cannot phonate, but demonstrates full comprehension by nodding, shaking head, writing, and following complex commands. He is neurologically intact (V1 - intubated/tracheostomy/anatomically unable to speak is typically scored as 1 if the scale requires verbalization).
- Motor response: Patient follows complex written and gestured commands, squeezes fingers, lifts arms, flexes hips, and moves all extremities purposefully without delay (M6)."}

step_info.output:
Glasgow Coma Scale (GCS) Calculation:
- Eye Opening (E): 4 points (Spontaneous/tracking)
- Verbal Response (V): 1 point (Unable to phonate due to total laryngectomy)
- Motor Response (M): 6 points (Follows commands, purposeful movement)

Calculation: 4 + 1 + 6 = 11

Final GCS Score: 11

step_info.thought: The patient is neurologically intact, but the standard Glasgow Coma Scale (GCS) assigns a score of 1 to the verbal component for patients who are unable to speak due to anatomical reasons such as a laryngectomy, even when they are fully alert and communicative through other means. Based on the clinical assessment provided in the note, the eye opening (4), motor response (6), and verbal response (1) result in a total GCS score of 11.

step_info.output: Final result processed.

args: A 36-year-old female, suffering from SLE since 10 years with effects on her central nervous system, developed diabetes mellitus (DM) 9 years ago, triggered by her long-term steroid therapy for SLE. She was undergoing steroid treatment (6 mg/day) for SLE at a different hospital. She was 158 cm tall and weighed 91.6 kg. Her body mass index was 36.7, indicating 3° higher obesity. To manage DM, she was treated with metformin, and her HbA1c was controlled at 7.4%. Serum immuno-reactive insulin (IRI) and C-peptide immunoreactivity (CPR) levels were 13.8 μU/ml and 2.5 ng/ml, respectively. Both the markers were in normal range. Total cholesterol (T-chol), triglyceride (TG), high-density lipoprotein cholesterol (HDL-C), and low-density lipoprotein cholesterol (LDL-C) levels were 191 mg/dL, 86 mg/dL, 41 mg/dL, and 126 mg/dL, respectively. Her dyslipidemia was controlled by administering atorvastatin. She had no hypertension as a complication of obesity. She was also treated with paroxetine hydrochloride hydrate, mianserin hydrochloride, and sodium valproate for steroid-induced depression. She could not work and depended on welfare services. To improve her obesity and DM, physicians suggested that she should undergo bariatric surgery in our hospital. She understood bariatric surgery well, and the symptoms of SLE were well controlled and stable, and she had no symptoms of central nervous system lupus. Anti-DNA and anti-Sm antibody levels were > 2.0 IU/ml and 2.5 U/ml, respectively. Both the SLE markers were in normal range. CH50, C3, and C4 levels were 53.8 U/ml, 144 mg/dL, and 26 mg/. All the SLE markers were in normal range, and SLE activity was well controlled as per laboratory data. She was given a diet instruction by her previous doctor but was unable to lose weight. Her obesity was considered to include an element of secondary obesity due to steroids. However, there were several studies reporting that patients with SLE who were obese were able to reduce their steroid dose along with reduction in their weight after bariatric surgery. Therefore, this case was judged to be an indication for bariatric surgery. Preoperative weight loss techniques were demonstrated at our outpatient clinic. She was treated with Mazindol and given diet instruction by a dietitian. She was able to lose 7 kg while continuing nutritional guidance for approximately 5 months. She was admitted in our hospital a week before the surgery, and her blood glucose was controlled. Thereafter, she underwent LSG, which lasted for 185 min, with a minimal blood loss and without complications. For the perioperative steroid cover, 200 mg of hydrocortisone sodium succinate was administered immediately before the surgery, and the maintenance dose (6 mg/day) was resumed on the first postoperative day. She was discharged on the eighth postoperative day without complications. Her blood glucose level stabilized immediately after the surgery; hence, her antidiabetic medication was stopped. After being discharged, she experienced gradual weight loss. After a year, she weighed 54.4 kg, losing approximately 37 kg, compared with her weight during the initial examination; additionally, rebound effects were not manifested (Fig. a). Body fat mass was also markedly reduced 1 year after the surgery compared with her preoperative status; meanwhile, her skeletal muscle mass was maintained (Fig. ). Furthermore, SLE condition was stable, and her steroid requirement was reduced to 4 mg/day (Fig. c). After surgery, her blood glucose level had settled down; thus, her DM drug therapy was halted. Her DM improved up to 5.7% of HbA1c 1 year after the surgery (Fig. b). Her dyslipidemia was well controlled by atorvastatin. T-chol, TG, HDL-C, and LDL-C levels were 165 mg/dL, 43 mg/dL, 48 mg/dL, and 104 mg/dL, respectively. For her steroid-induced depression, paroxetine hydrochloride hydrate and mianserin hydrochloride were reduced by half, and sodium valproate medication was terminated 9 months after surgery. Through weight loss, she could begin to work and be part of the society again.

What is the patient's body surface area? Please output your answer in terms of m². You should use the patient's medical values and health status when they were first admitted to the hospital prior to any treatment.

step_info.args: {"focus": "Calculate Body Surface Area (BSA) using the Mosteller formula for a patient who was 158 cm tall and 91.6 kg when first admitted."}

step_info.output:
Clinical Calculation: Body Surface Area (BSA)
Extracted Variables:
- Height: 158 cm
- Weight: 91.6 kg

Formula Applied:
Mosteller Formula: BSA (m²) = sqrt((height (cm) * weight (kg)) / 3600)

Step-by-Step Arithmetic:
1. Multiply height by weight: 158 * 91.6 = 14,472.8
2. Divide the result by 3600: 14,472.8 / 3600 ≈ 4.0202
3. Calculate the square root: sqrt(4.0202) ≈ 2.005

Final Result:
2.01 m²

step_info.thought: The patient's initial height and weight were extracted from the medical note, and the Mosteller formula was applied to calculate the body surface area (BSA). The calculation yields a result of 2.01 m².

step_info.output: Final result processed.

args:
In January 2013, a 65-year-old Japanese woman was admitted to our hospital for the evaluation of renal dysfunction. In 2005, when she was 56 years old, SS was diagnosed because of a positive Schirmer/Rose Bengal test and the detection of anti-SS-A antibody at a titer of 1:16. A limited form of SSc was also diagnosed at that time because of a thickening of the finger skin, Raynaud phenomenon, and positivity for anti-centromere antibody (titer of 1:167). Thereafter, she was followed up without any medication. In April 2012, serum creatinine was 1.0 mg/dl and urinalysis was normal.
On admission, the patient was 155 cm tall and weighed 48.2 kg, with a blood pressure of 139/70 mm Hg and a temperature of 36.5°C. Her serum creatinine was 1.6 mg/dl, proteinuria was 1.6 g daily, and the urine sediment contained 20–29 erythrocytes/high-power field. ANCA was measured by enzyme-linked immunosorbent assay. It was positive for myeloperoxidase (MPO) at 130 enzyme-linked immunosorbent assay units (EU)/ml (normal <10 EU/ml), but was negative for anti-proteinase-3 and anti-glomerular basement membrane (GBM) antibodies. Antinuclear antibody was positive at a titer of 1:1280, anti-centromere antibody was positive at a titer of 167.0 U/ml (normal; <10.0), anti-SS-A antibody at a titer of 1:16, and anti-SS-B antibody was positive at a titer of 1:8. However, double-stranded-DNA, Smith, U1-nuclear ribonucleoprotein (RNP), RNA-polymerase III, rheumatoid factor, cyclic citrullinated peptide, and scleroderma (Scl)-70 antibodies were all negative.
Interstitial lung disease was noted by CT, but there was no neurological or skin involvement.
A light microscopic examination of a renal biopsy specimen containing 23 glomeruli revealed a global or segmental sclerosis in 5 glomeruli and necrotizing glomerulonephritis with fibrin deposition in 2 glomeruli (fig. ), but definite crescent formation was not noted. Focal interstitial lymphoplasmacytic infiltration was also seen (fig. ). There was no edematous narrowing or obliteration of renal interlobular arteries and arterioles. Immunofluorescence microscopy did not demonstrate any deposits of immunoglobulin or complement, and there were no electron-dense deposits on electron microscopy. ANCA-related necrotizing glomerulonephritis and focal interstitial nephritis were diagnosed, and tubulointerstitial nephritis was considered to have a close association with SS. However, SSc-related small arterial lesions were not noted.
Her treatment was initiated with intravenous methylprednisolone pulse therapy (1,000 mg/day for 3 days), followed by prednisolone (PSL) at 40 mg/day. The MPO-ANCA titer became normal within 6 months of starting treatment, and PSL was gradually tapered to 10 mg daily. Proteinuria decreased to 0.3 g daily, but serum creatinine remained at 1.6 mg/dl. One year after starting treatment, PSL was tapered to 5 mg daily and azathioprine was added at 50 mg daily. Urinalysis remained almost normal.

Using the Ideal Body Weight Formula, what is the patient's ideal body weight in terms of kg? You should use the patient's medical values and health status when they were first admitted to the hospital prior to any treatment.

step_info.args: {"focus": "Calculate Ideal Body Weight (IBW) for a 65-year-old female who is 155 cm tall (Devine formula: IBW for females = 45.5 kg + 2.3 kg * (height in inches - 60)). Note: 155 cm is 61.02 inches."}

step_info.output:
Clinical Calculation Summary: Ideal Body Weight (IBW)

Extracted Variables:
- Patient Gender: Female
- Patient Height: 155 cm (61.02 inches)

Formula Applied:
Devine Formula (Females): IBW = 45.5 kg + 2.3 kg * (Height in inches - 60)

Step-by-Step Arithmetic:
1. Calculate height adjustment: 61.02 inches - 60 = 1.02 inches
2. Calculate weight adjustment: 1.02 inches * 2.3 kg/inch = 2.346 kg
3. Calculate base weight: 45.5 kg + 2.346 kg = 47.846 kg

Final Result:
47.85 kg
Final result processed.

args: A 46-year-old man with a background of long-standing type 2 diabetes complicated by stage-2 chronic kidney disease (baseline serum creatinine 1.2 mg/dL recorded at a primary-care visit three months ago) and chronic plaque psoriasis treated intermittently with methotrexate was brought to the emergency department by co-workers after collapsing at a construction site. According to witnesses he had sliced the anterior aspect of his right lower leg with a hand-held angle grinder two days earlier, self-applied gauze and duct tape, continued working through escalating pain, and drank little except caffeinated soda because of a tight deadline. During the thirty-minute ride in a privately owned pickup, he remained upright on the passenger seat, breathing through his mouth with shallow respirations but speaking coherently; no ambulance crew was involved, so prior to arrival he had received neither intravenous fluids, antibiotics, analgesics, vasoactive medications, nor any form of positive-pressure breathing assistance. On rolling straight from the vehicle to triage—before any hospital therapy—initial vital signs documented an oral temperature of 38.9 °C, pulse 118 beats min⁻¹ in regular sinus rhythm, respiratory rate 24 min⁻¹ with mild accessory-muscle use, and an automated blood-pressure reading of 124 / 79 mm Hg that was corroborated by a manual measurement on the opposite arm. A pulse-oximeter on room air showed 92 % saturation; nursing staff placed a 2 L min⁻¹ nasal cannula that the departmental analyzer verified as delivering approximately 0.28 fraction of inspired oxygen. Five minutes later a radial arterial sample—drawn while he remained on that low-flow interface—revealed pH 7.33, PaCO₂ 36 mm Hg, bicarbonate 19 mEq L⁻¹, lactate 3.6 mmol L⁻¹, and a PaO₂ of 148 mm Hg on the recorded 28 % FiO₂. Neurologic evaluation done prior to any sedative or opioid showed pupils 3 mm brisk, eyes opening to voice, confused conversation that supplied his correct name and location but stated the wrong year, and purposeful withdrawal of both upper limbs to nail-bed pressure, yielding a composite Glasgow Coma Score of 14. Skin examination disclosed a pale, diaphoretic face, multiple psoriatic plaques on the elbows, and a 6-cm longitudinal laceration over the right pretibial area surrounded by violaceous edema and crepitus extending to mid-calf with scant serosanguinous ooze but no foul odor; the surrounding tissue was exquisitely tender and warm to touch. Capillary refill at the fingertips was two seconds, extremities were warm, and jugular venous distention was absent at 45°. Breath sounds were clear bilaterally without crackles or wheeze, and chest expansion appeared symmetric. Cardiac auscultation disclosed a rapid but regular first and second heart sound without murmurs, rubs, or gallops. The abdomen was soft, nondistended, and non-tender with normal bowel sounds. A 16-gauge antecubital line was placed for laboratory studies before any crystalloid bolus was started. Venous blood drawn simultaneously with the arterial gas demonstrated sodium 136 mmol L⁻¹, potassium 5.0 mmol L⁻¹, chloride 102 mmol L⁻¹, glucose 212 mg dL⁻¹, blood urea nitrogen 38 mg dL⁻¹, and a serum creatinine of 2.0 mg dL⁻¹, confirming acute worsening from baseline. Complete blood count revealed leukocytes 18.7 × 10³ µL⁻¹ with 14 % band forms, hemoglobin 13.2 g dL⁻¹, hematocrit 39 %, and a platelet concentration of 107 000 / µL. Hepatic panel showed aspartate aminotransferase 48 U L⁻¹, alanine aminotransferase 46 U L⁻¹, alkaline phosphatase 112 U L⁻¹, international normalized ratio 1.1, and a total bilirubin of 0.95 mg dL⁻¹. High-sensitivity troponin I was <0.01 ng mL⁻¹. A portable anterior-posterior chest radiograph obtained while he continued to breathe unaided through the nasal cannula demonstrated clear lungs and normal cardiac silhouette, and a point-of-care ultrasound of the affected limb displayed subcutaneous gas with fascial fluid tracks but preserved deep venous flow. Bladder scan estimated 220 mL of urine; on insertion, a 14-French Foley drained 90 mL of clear yellow fluid, and his partner, who tracks health parameters on a smartphone application, reported summed voids of approximately 1 550 mL during the previous twenty-four hours despite reduced oral intake. Twelve-lead electrocardiography printed immediately after arterial cannulation showed sinus tachycardia at 118 beats min⁻¹ with nonspecific ST-segment changes but no ischemia. Up to this exact moment—prior to administration of crystalloid, broad-spectrum antibiotics, surgical consultation, analgesia, or vasopressors—the patient maintained spontaneous breathing on low-flow oxygen without any mask or mechanical assistance, preserved an arterial pressure of 124 / 79 mm Hg, exhibited moderate thrombocytopenia at 107 000 / µL, held a normal total bilirubin of 0.95 mg dL⁻¹, manifested acute kidney injury evidenced by creatinine 2.0 mg dL⁻¹ in the context of non-oliguric urine output tallying 1 550 mL in one day, and demonstrated mild encephalopathy quantified by a Glasgow score of 14 while displaying clinical and imaging features concerning for evolving necrotizing soft-tissue infection of the right lower leg with systemic inflammatory response.
What is the patient's Sequential Organ Failure Assessment (SOFA) Score?

step_info.args: {"focus": "Calculate the Sequential Organ Failure Assessment (SOFA) score for the patient described in the note. The patient is a 46-year-old male. Key data points: PaO2=148 mmHg, FiO2=0.28 (PaO2/FiO2 ratio calculation needed), GCS=14, MAP=94 mmHg (calculated from 124/79), creatinine=2.0 mg/dL, bilirubin=0.95 mg/dL, platelets=107,000/uL."}

step_info.output:
Sequential Organ Failure Assessment (SOFA) Score Report:

Variables Identified:
- Respiration (PaO2/FiO2: 148 / 0.28 = 528.6 mmHg): 0 points
- Coagulation (Platelets: 107,000/uL): 1 point
- Liver (Bilirubin: 0.95 mg/dL): 0 points
- Cardiovascular (MAP: 94 mmHg): 0 points
- Central Nervous System (GCS: 14): 1 point
- Renal (Creatinine: 2.0 mg/dL): 2 points

Calculation:
0 + 1 + 0 + 0 + 1 + 2 = 4

Final SOFA Score: 4

step_info.thought: The patient's SOFA score has been calculated based on the provided clinical data, identifying specific organ dysfunctions: 1 point for platelets, 1 point for GCS, and 2 points for creatinine, resulting in a total score of 4. I have verified the arithmetic and variable mapping, and the final result is 4.

step_info.output: Final result processed.

args: A patient has a heart rate of 61 bpm and a QT interval of 278 msec.
Using the Fridericia Formula for corrected QT interval, what is the patient's corrected QT interval in terms of msec?

step_info.args: {"focus": "Calculate QTc using Fridericia's formula for a patient with HR 61 bpm and QT 278 msec."}

step_info.output:
Clinical Score Calculation Report: QTc (Fridericia)

1. Required Variables:
   - QT interval (msec)
   - Heart Rate (HR, bpm)

2. Extracted Values:
   - QT: 278 msec
   - HR: 61 bpm

3. Formula Used:
   - QTc = QT / (RR)^(1/3)
   - Note: RR (in seconds) = 60 / HR

4. Substitution and Arithmetic:
   - Step 1 (RR interval): 60 / 61 = 0.9836 s
   - Step 2 (Cube root of RR): (0.9836)^(1/3) = 0.9945
   - Step 3 (Convert QT to s): 278 / 1000 = 0.278 s
   - Step 4 (Calculate QTc): 0.278 / 0.9945 = 0.2795 s
   - Step 5 (Convert to msec): 0.2795 * 1000 = 279.5 msec

5. Final Result:
   - QTc (Fridericia) = 279.5 msec

step_info.thought: The calculation of the corrected QT interval using the Fridericia formula has been performed based on the provided patient note. The final result of 279.5 msec has been derived through standard clinical calculations, and I am now ready to return this value.

step_info.output: Final result processed.

calculator_name: CHA2DS2-VASc Score for Atrial Fibrillation Stroke Risk

args:
In March 2016 a 77-year-old Italian man attended the Emergency Room of San Giuseppe Hospital in Empoli. He was a retired farmer, who had been suffering from hematochezia together with melena for two weeks. His vital parameters were normal. In his medical history there was chronic atrial fibrillation, for which he was taking rivaroxaban 20 mg/d and digoxin 0.125 mg/d, and prior cholecystectomy. He weighed 68 kg, was 170 cm tall (body mass index 23.5). Digital rectal examination revealed the presence of melena. A nasogastric tube was inserted, with no evidence of gastric blood traces. Laboratory tests showed anemia (Hb 5.1 g/dL). The patient underwent blood transfusions and esophagogastroduodenoscopy (EGD), which showed a small hiatal hernia, bile in the stomach and a 10 mm duodenal fibrinous ulcer. We decided to suspend rivaroxaban and start low molecular weight heparin. Continuous intravenous infusion of pantoprazole was administered and the patient was admitted to the Gastroenterology Department.
In the following days he continued to bleed and required further blood transfusions. He also underwent several EGDs, which confirmed the previous findings and allowed us to get biopsies in the antrum for rapid urease test and on the ulcer margins for histology assessment. We also used argon plasma coagulation on the oozing borders of the ulcer, with consequent bleeding cessation. The rapid urease test was positive for H. pylori and the patient started eradication treatment. The biopsies of the ulcer margins demonstrated regenerative hyperplasia on productive inflammation.
Despite endoscopic therapy, the patient still complained of melena and hematochezia. He underwent ileocolonoscopy, which showed bright red blood in ileum and colon, without identification of the bleeding source. We performed a contrast-enhanced computed tomography (CT) scan of the abdomen and of the abdominal aorta, which showed no bleeding cause and only minor findings like parietal calcifications of blood vessels, a small hepatic cyst, diffuse moderate intra- and extrahepatic biliary ducts dilation and benign prostatic hyperplasia. We then decided to use wireless capsule endoscopy (WCE), which described two duodenal fibrinous ulcers (5 and 10 mm) without signs of recent bleeding, the presence of a tapeworm, starting from 1 h 45 min until 4 h 28 min after WCE ingestion (Figures and ) and plentiful dark red blood in the colon, lighter in the proximal regions.
The patient reported only then, that he had occasionally eaten raw beef and that he had taken mebendazole 5 mo before, because of suspected oxyuriasis. The patient received albendazole 400 mg/d for five days, since this drug was suitable for taeniasis and already available in the department.
While on albendazole, he still complained about melena and iron-deficiency anemia (IDA) (Hb 9 g/dL, Hct 27%, RDW-CV 17%, iron 27 μg/dL, ferritin 53 ng/mL), with constant need of blood transfusions. He also stated he had not discharged the head of the tapeworm yet. We performed the last EGD, which demonstrated healing of the ulcers, with no bleeding signs. The second ileocolonoscopy was comparable to the first one, hence we decided to give him a second WCE after discontinuation of albendazole, which demonstrated persistence of the tapeworm starting from jejunum to the whole ileum (last visualization at 4 h 25 min) and plentiful dark red blood in the colon, lighter in the proximal regions (Figures and ).
We consulted an infectious disease specialist for a change of therapy after albendazole failure. He advised to give the patient niclosamide 2 g in a single administration and then to initiate bowel preparation with macrogol. After niclosamide and bowel preparation we performed the last ileocolonoscopy, which displayed no blood in ileum or colon and 2 non polypoid lesions in the caecum that we removed via loop electrosurgical excision procedure. Histology revealed those lesions to be intestinal tubular adenomas with low grade dysplasia.
The day after niclosamide administration, the patient felt a lot better. Melena and rectal bleeding had finally stopped and after two more days he noticed the complete excretion of the tapeworm head. Since his condition was improving, we decided to dismiss him.
He was seen at follow-up visit in mid-June 2016, where he stated he was feeling good and had no more rectal bleeding. He performed fecal obscure blood test and H. pylori stool antigen test, both showing negative results. His hemoglobin had reached 11.3 g/dL by then, with a positive trend. He underwent ova and parasite fecal exams at 2, 4 and 6 mo after dismission, none of which showed relapse of the infestation.
What is the patient's CHA2DS2-VASc Score? You should use the patient's medical values and health status when they were first admitted to the hospital prior to any treatment.

step_info.output:

No other relevant comorbidities (such as hypertension, heart failure, diabetes, or history of stroke/TIA) were mentioned in his history upon admission. Therefore, the total score is 2.

args:
The 26-year-old Chinese lady was an immigrant from the Inner Mongolia and incidentally found in the 8th week of pregnancy. She had being lived on cow milk products for 24 years in Inner Mongolia before she married a man in Guilin. As an aboriginal inhabitant, fresh milk and milk products were her favorite. She had 1-year history of renal biopsy-proven MGN with persistent proteinuria (2.5–5.5 g/24 hours). The lady was admitted to the Affiliated Hospital of Guilin Medical University in March 2014.
The patient was first admitted to the People's Hospital of Guilin with complaints of generalized edema in her face and legs in March 2013. No other abnormalities had previously been detected on her annual physical examinations.
The results of physical examination revealed normal, except for 2+ pitting pretibial edema and lower eyelids swelling. Laboratory measurements were as follows: total serum protein, 44.8 g/L (normal 60–80 g/L); albumin, 24.6 g/L (normal 35–50 g/L); total cholesterol, 7.91 mmol/L (normal 3.1–6.0 mmol/L), triglycerides, 1.22 mmol/L (normal 0.48–1.6 mmol/L), high-density lipoprotein, 1.49 mmol/L (normal 1.0–1.9 mmol/L); and low-density lipoprotein, 4.43 mmol/L (normal 2.1–3.36 mmol/L). The levels of liver enzymes, blood electrolytes, and the results of blood routine examination were basically within the normal ranges. Urinalysis showed the presence of urine dipstick protein of 3+, 24-hour urine protein level of 5.5 g/day (normal 0.04–0.23), blood urea nitrogen of 2.65 mmol/L (normal 3.2–7.5 mmol/L), and creatinine of 55.5 μmol/L (normal 44–115 μmol/L).
She was hospitalized to have percutaneous kidney biopsy performed on the 21st March, 2013. Renal tissues were obtained by core needle biopsy under ultrasound control on the basis of written informed consent from the patient. One third each of the renal biopsy specimen was processed for histochemical stains (HE, periodic acid-Schiff [PAS], periodic Schiff-methenamine, and Masson trichrome) with light microscopy; immunofluorescence stains for the monoclonal IgG, IgA, IgM, C1q, and C3c; and ultrastructural examination with transmission electron microscopy (Supplementary methods).
PAS stain with light microscopy showed diffused thickening of GBM (Figs. –). Mesangial expansion, proliferation, and sclerosis, and hyalinosis of glomerular arterioles and intralobular arteries were not significant.
Silver stain of periodic Schiff-methenamine highlighted the irregular GBM thickening due to subepithelial deposits to form spikes (Figs. –). The spikes were not evident by stains of HE, PAS, and Masson trichrome.
Immunofluorescence microscopy demonstrated strong global granular GBM reactivity for IgG (3+, Fig. ). Immunofluorescence for IgA, IgM, C1q, and C3c was not significant.
Electron microscopy pinpointed diffuse, irregular, and numerous electron-dense deposits along the GBM in subepithelial spaces (Fig. ).
Tubular changes mainly occurred in the proximal convoluted tubules and were characterized by loss of tubular brush border and formation of lipid peroxidation (Fig. ). Characteristic changes in interstitial compartments were lipid-laden foam cell infiltration (Fig. ).

args:
A 57-year-old man visited Inje University Ilsan Paik Hospital after experiencing abdominal pain, nausea, and vomiting for 3 weeks. He had visited our emergency room for the same symptoms 2 weeks prior, where he was suspected of having paralytic ileus. However, he was discharged against our medical advice, insisting that his symptoms were not severe. His symptoms had not improved since this visit. He had chronic renal disease for 10 years. Instead of taking prescription drugs, he had been using self-manufactured herbal medication for over 6 months. The herbal medication included more than 10 herbal ingredients such as sansisi (Gardenia jasminoides), hua shi (magnesium silicate), long gu (Mastodi ossis fossilia or talc), and mu li (Ostrea gigas). He had been taking no other medication except for the herbal medicine. He was a 15-pack-year current smoker, and did not drink alcohol.
The patient looked acutely ill; his blood pressure was 132/71 mmHg, pulse rate 97/min, respiratory rate 20/min, and body temperature 36.2℃. The patient's abdomen was distended and tympanic, and showed tenderness without rebound tenderness. In addition, his bowel sounds were decreased. Laboratory tests were as follows: white blood cell 6,970/mm3, hemoglobin 8.1 g/dL, platelet count 372,000/mm3, BUN 43 mg/dL, creatinine 4.52 mg/dL, and CRP 0.3 mg/dL. The electrolyte levels were within normal limits.
A plain radiograph showed diffuse small bowel distension and colon wall calcification along the colonic area (). Because of his reduced renal function, a non-contrast-enhanced CT scan was taken, which showed diffuse fluid-filled distension of the small bowel with colonic wall thickening and calcifications in the colon wall and at the mesocolon, suggesting atherosclerotic changes (). Paralytic ileus was suspected, and the patient was initially managed with supportive care including hydration, bowel rest, and nasogastric drainage. However, a follow-up CT taken on hospital day 7 did not show any improvement in bowel distention. Colonoscopy without bowel preparation performed on hospital day 8 showed diffuse bluish edematous mucosal change, multiple scarring, and loss of vascularity from the distal sigmoid to the transverse colon. There was a pinpoint stricture in the transverse colon, 65 cm from the anus, and scope passage was not possible (). A biopsy taken from the stricture site showed chronic inflammation and fibrosis with mild regenerative change (). Under the supposition of a PC diagnosis, a contrast-enhanced CT scan with delayed phase angiography was performed, which showed extensive calcifications along the colonic wall, mesenteric, and portal veins (). Total colectomy was recommended, but the patient refused. Fortunately, on the day after the colonoscopy, the patient's symptoms dramatically improved, and the ileus disappeared (). Presumably, this was due to the dilatational effect from the scope passage attempt, or dislodgement of a fecalith. Based on radiologic, endoscopic, and histologic findings, we diagnosed the patient with PC. After several days of a soft diet, the patient remained asymptomatic, and was discharged. The patient is currently symptom-free, after 2 months of follow-up.

args: A 62-year-old man with a remote history of stage-IV laryngeal squamous cell carcinoma treated eight years ago by total laryngectomy with a permanent tracheal stoma, coronary artery disease status post drug-eluting stent, and well-controlled type 2 diabetes managed on metformin alone was transported to our emergency department after his spouse discovered him at 07:18 lying face-down on the floor of their detached workshop where a gasoline-powered generator had been running behind closed doors; she last interacted with him roughly twenty minutes prior while he was inspecting power tools, heard an abrupt thud, and, on entering, noted an acrid exhaust odor, no seizure activity, and no obvious trauma. County fire-rescue arrived nine minutes later, disconnected the generator, moved the patient to fresh air, and placed a non-rebreather mask delivering 15 L/min oxygen over the tracheal stoma with an improvised pediatric mask seal; a single 18-gauge catheter was inserted in the left antecubital vein and flushed with 5 mL normal saline, but in order to preserve the neurologic examination no crystalloid bolus, opioid, benzodiazepine, sedative, dextrose, vasopressor, antihypertensive, anticonvulsant, hyperosmolar agent, or paralytic was given during the eleven-minute ground transport that followed. Prehospital capillary glucose registered 112 mg/dL and portable co-oximetry could not be obtained in the field. On arrival at 07:40—before any hospital intervention—simultaneous automated vital signs recorded blood pressure 146/84 mm Hg in the right brachial artery, heart rate 92 beats/min in regular sinus rhythm, respiratory rate 20 breaths/min with shallow diaphragmatic excursion through the stoma mask, oxygen saturation 100 % on the delivered flow, and a tympanic temperature of 36.9 °C. The airway exam revealed a clean, mature anterior cervical stoma emitting warm humidified breath with coarse voice prosthesis in situ, no dried secretions, no soot, and an intact cough when a soft suction catheter gently contacted the tracheal mucosa; oropharyngeal inspection showed edentulous gums, midline tongue, moist mucosa, and no blood or emesis. Pupils were 3 mm bilaterally, round, and briskly reactive to both direct and consensual light; corneal reflexes and spontaneous blink were intact, and funduscopic study deferred given the urgency. For twenty seconds of silent observation under bright LEDs, eyelids remained gently closed, lashes motionless. When his surname was spoken twice in a firm conversational tone near the right ear, both lids promptly parted to half aperture, hazel irides tracked toward the voice, and he maintained gaze for several seconds before the lids drifted shut; repetition of verbal prompting reliably reproduced lid opening, whereas firm trapezius squeeze alone failed to elicit ocular response. Because of the prior laryngectomy he produced no phonation at baseline, and multiple attempts to elicit speech by asking yes/no questions, prompting him to articulate his name, or requesting him to count resulted in complete silence—no guttural sounds, no whispered air, and no mouthing of words—though facial expression remained attentive. Limb examination revealed normal bulk and low-normal resting tone without abnormal posturing. On command to “show two fingers,” he extended the index and middle digits of the right hand accurately; when told “wiggle your left toes,” he performed the movement briskly; and with the directive “lift both arms,” he raised each to 90 degrees and held for five seconds without drift. These combined findings correspond to a Glasgow Coma Scale of 10T, reflecting eye opening to verbal command, absence of verbal output, and full obedience to motor commands. Painful stimuli were deliberately avoided once consistent cooperation with simple commands was demonstrated. Deep tendon reflexes measured 2 + and symmetric at biceps, triceps, patellae, and Achilles, and plantar responses were flexor bilaterally. There was no nuchal rigidity, and cranial nerve testing otherwise revealed symmetric facial musculature, midline tongue when protruded, and intact shoulder shrug. Cardiovascular examination showed a regular S1 and S2 without murmurs, rubs, or gallops; distal pulses were 2 + and equal with capillary refill under two seconds. Breath sounds were equal and vesicular bilaterally with intermittent coarse upper airway turbulence transmitted from the stoma, percussion resonant, no wheeze or rales. Abdomen was flat, soft, nontender, and nondistended with active bowel sounds; pelvis was stable to gentle compression, and rectal tone was present with no gross blood. Skin survey revealed faint erythema over the anterior chest where the oxygen mask strap had rested, but no contusions, abrasions, lacerations, or step-offs were detected along the scalp, spine, or long bones. Portable anterior-posterior chest radiograph performed within five minutes demonstrated clear lung fields, normal cardiac silhouette, and the tracheal stoma device in expected position. An arterial blood gas obtained on current oxygen flow showed pH 7.38, PaCO₂ 39 mm Hg, PaO₂ 310 mm Hg, bicarbonate 23 mEq/L, and carboxyhemoglobin level pending; rapid serum chemistry returned sodium 138 mEq/L, potassium 4.2 mEq/L, chloride 103 mEq/L, bicarbonate 24 mEq/L, blood urea nitrogen 18 mg/dL, creatinine 0.9 mg/dL, and glucose 114 mg/dL, while initial troponin I was below the detection limit. To this moment he has received no intravenous fluid boluses, analgesics, sedatives, neuromuscular blockers, vasopressors, antidotes, or hyperbaric therapy, ensuring that the eyelid opening only to spoken address, the complete absence of vocalization despite an apparently patent airway prosthesis, and the consistent ability to perform requested limb movements accurately reflect his untreated neurologic status at presentation as toxicology, hyperbaric medicine, and otolaryngology teams assemble for further management, including planned CT brain without contrast and arterial blood co-oximetry confirmation of suspected carbon monoxide exposure.

args: A patient has a heart rate of 123 bpm and a QT interval of 265 msec.
Using the Hodges Formula for corrected QT interval, what is the patient's corrected QT interval in terms of msec?

step_info.args: {"focus": "Calculate QTc using the Hodges formula for a patient with HR of 123 bpm and QT interval of 265 msec."}

step_info.output:
Clinical Variables Identified:
- Heart Rate (HR): 123 bpm
- QT Interval (QT): 265 msec

Formula Used:
Hodges Formula: QTc = QT + 1.75 * (HR - 60)

Calculation Steps:
1. Substitute values: QTc = 265 + 1.75 * (123 - 60)
2. Calculate HR - 60: 123 - 60 = 63
3. Multiply by correction factor: 1.75 * 63 = 110.25
4. Add to base QT interval: 265 + 110.25 = 375.25

Final Result:
QTc = 375.25 msec

step_info.thought: The clinical tool successfully extracted the patient's heart rate and QT interval, applied the Hodges formula (QTc = QT + 1.75 * (HR - 60)), and calculated the result step-by-step. The final corrected QT interval is 375.25 msec.

step_info.output: Final result processed.

args: A patient has a heart rate of 165 bpm and a QT interval of 474 msec.
Using the Bazett Formula for corrected QT interval, what is the patient's corrected QT interval in terms of msec?

step_info.args: {"focus": "Calculate QTc using Bazett's formula for a patient with HR 165 bpm and QT interval 474 msec."}

step_info.output:
Clinical Calculation Report: QTc (Bazett's Formula)

1. Identified Variables:
   - Heart Rate (HR): 165 bpm
   - QT Interval: 474 msec

2. Formula Used:
   Bazett's Formula: QTc = QT / sqrt(RR)
   (Where RR = 60 / HR in seconds)

3. Substitution:
   - RR = 60 / 165 = 0.3636 s
   - QTc = 474 / sqrt(0.3636)

4. Step-by-Step Arithmetic:
   a. Calculate RR interval: 60 / 165 = 0.3636
   b. Calculate square root of RR: sqrt(0.3636) = 0.6030
   c. Calculate QTc: 474 / 0.6030 = 786.07

5. Final Result:
   786.07 msec

step_info.thought: The clinical tool successfully calculated the corrected QT interval using Bazett's formula based on the heart rate and QT interval provided in the note. The calculation steps are clearly laid out, and the final value is ready to be returned as the output of the function.

step_info.output: Final result processed.

args:
A 59-year-old Hispanic male from Galveston, Texas presented to the Emergency Department on April 29, 2012 with complaints of headache and fever. His headache started two weeks prior, and he noted temperatures up to 40 degrees Celsius in the preceding five days. He denied neck pain, visual complaints, respiratory symptoms, or arthralgias. He denied recent travel, eating unpasteurized milk products, and sexual activity for the last several years. Two days prior to presentation, he was evaluated by the Emergency Department and was discharged with a presumed viral illness. Because of persistent fever and headache, he returned to the Emergency Department where he was admitted for further evaluation.
His medical history was significant for open-angle glaucoma and seasonal allergies. Outpatient medications included travoprost and dorzolamide ophthalmic drops. Multiple family members had diabetes mellitus and hypertension, and his father had a diagnosis of leukemia. He denied use of tobacco or illicit drugs. He drank alcohol rarely.
On examination, his temperature was 39.2 degrees Celsius, blood pressure 117/78 mmHg, heart rate 75 beats/minute, respiratory rate 16 breaths/minute, and oxygen saturation 95% on ambient air. He appeared ill, and skin examination was remarkable for a lightly erythematous macular rash on his chest and arms (). The examination was otherwise unremarkable.
Laboratory data revealed a leukocyte count of 3700/μL (3500 to 10,000/μL), erythrocyte sedimentation rate of 15 mm/hour (0 to 10 mm/hour), C-reactive protein of 12.3 mg/dL (0.0 to 0.8 mg/dL), ferritin of 1320 ng/mL (18 to 464 ng/mL), aspartate aminotransferase of 66 units/L (13 to 40 units/L), and alanine transaminase of 94 units/L (9 to 51 units/L). Serologic tests for viral hepatitis, HIV, cytomegalovirus, Epstein-Barr virus, syphilis, and Bartonella were nonreactive. Anti-nuclear antibody, antibodies to double-stranded DNA, and rheumatoid factor were negative. Blood and urine cultures were negative. Cerebrospinal fluid analysis was unremarkable. Chest radiograph and computed tomography of the head, abdomen, and pelvis were negative for lesions suggestive of malignancy or focal infectious process.
Throughout hospitalization, the patient continued to have daily fevers with temperatures up to 40 degrees Celsius. A careful revisitation of his history revealed exposure to opossums around his yard and home. This historical information in combination of his symptoms, rash, and mild transaminitis rendered murine typhus a likely diagnosis. Doxycycline was initiated and was followed by complete resolution of his symptoms within 48 hours. Although an immunofluorescence IgG assay for typhus group antibodies was nonreactive, the patient was discharged to complete a seven-day course of doxycycline.
One month after discharge, the patient returned to clinic and was free of symptoms. Serum obtained for an immunofluorescence IgG assay for typhus group antibodies was reactive at a dilution of 1 : 1024, confirming the diagnosis of murine typhus.

A 76-year-old man with a past medical history of prostate cancer, paroxysmal atrial fibrillation, and recurring pneumonia was found to have a 10-mm nodule in the left lower lung without hilar or mediastinal lymphadenopathy on CT of the chest with IV contrast (Figure ). CT/PET scan showed abnormal uptake in the lung nodule (Figure ) and grade II cervical lymphadenopathy. CT-guided biopsy of the left lower lung was performed. Histopathology showed a malignant neoplasm of plasmacytoid cells (Figure ). A panel of immunohistochemical stains was performed to further evaluate the lesion. The tumor cells were strongly and diffusely immunoreactive for the melanocytic marker MART-1 (Figure ). However, stains for AE1/AE3, CK7, TIF-1, Napsin, P63, PSA, and PASP were negative. The immunohistochemical results confirmed a diagnosis of metastatic melanoma of unknown primary origin (MUP) to the cervical lymph nodes (LNs) and lungs. The patient had just finished four cycles of ipilimumab when he presented to our clinic complaining of fatigue, generalized weakness, dizziness, nausea, abdominal pain, and a 10-pound weight loss. The patient denied any fever, chills, chest pain, shortness of breath, or diarrhea. He denied any smoking or drug-use history. Vital signs included a blood pressure of 98/60 mmHg, heart rate of 92 bpm, respiratory rate of 20 bpm, and oxygen saturation of 92% on room air. The physical exam was unremarkable except for a slightly enlarged left cervical LN, dark pigmentation of the lips and gingiva, and sinus tachycardia without abnormal heart sounds or murmurs. The complete blood count was within normal limits.
The labs before starting ipilimumab were as follows - sodium (Na): 140 mEq/L (normal range: 135-145 mEq/L), potassium: (K) 3.6 mEq/L (normal range: 3.5-5.2 mEq/L), chloride (Cl): 105 mEq/L (normal range: 96-106 mEq/L), carbon dioxide (CO2): 28 mEq/L (normal range: 23-29 mEq/L), blood urea nitrogen (BUN): 19 mg/dL (normal range: 6-20 mg/dL); creatinine (Cr): 1.3 mg/dL (normal range: 0.8-1.2 mg/dL); albumin: 3.9 g/dL, and glucose: 110 mg/dL (normal range: 64-100 mg/dL); morning cortisol: 17 μg/dL (normal range: 5-25 μg/dL), adrenocorticotropic hormone (ACTH): 14 pg/mL (normal range: <80 pg/mL), thyroid-stimulating hormone (TSH): 2.4 μU/mL (normal range: 0.4-5 μU/mL), and free thyroxin: 1.2 ng/dL (normal range: 0.8-2.8 ng/dL).
The labs after four cycles of ipilimumab were as follows - Na: 131 mEq/L, K: 4.1 mEq/L, Cl: 87 mEq/L, CO2: 27 mEq/L, BUN: 6 mg/dL, Cr: 1.1 mg/dL, albumin: 3.7 g/dL, and glucose: 89 mg/dL; morning cortisol: 5 μg/dL, ACTH: 120 pg/mL, TSH: 5 μU/mL, free thyroxin: 0.9 ng/dL, testosterone: 437 ng/dL (normal range: 270-1,070 ng/dL), follicle-stimulating hormone (FSH): 3.5 mIU/mL (normal range: 1.5-12.4 mIU/mL), luteinizing hormone (LH): 6.8 mIU/mL (normal range: 1.24-7.8 mIU/mL), prolactin: 11 ng/mL (normal range: 2-18 ng/mL). Aldosterone was undetectable, and renin was 31 ng/mL/h (normal range for normal sodium diet: 0.6-4.3 ng/mL/h). The patient's HbA1C was 5.7%, and blood culture showed no growth. Urinalysis showed no abnormality and procalcitonin was negative. After the administration of 250 μg intravenous (IV) cosyntropin, cortisol was found to be 6.8 μg/dL at 30 mins, and 10.2 μg/dL at 60 mins, confirming the diagnosis of PAI.
He was diagnosed with grade 3 toxicity of ipilimumab and, given his severe symptoms limiting his daily activities, was hospitalized. The patient was immediately started on IV fluid resuscitation and hydrocortisone 100 mg IV bolus, followed by 50 mg IV every six hours. A CT of the abdomen and pelvis with and without IV contrast revealed no evidence of intraabdominal or pelvic pathology. MRI of the brain with and without contrast revealed no evidence of hypophysitis and no evidence of intracranial metastatic disease. Symptomatic improvement was seen at 24 hours. He was discharged with a four-day taper of hydrocortisone as per the recommendations of the endocrinologist. On his follow-up at the endocrinology clinic, his symptoms continued to improve. A repeat cosyntropin stimulation test showed cortisol levels within normal limits. The patient was started on a second checkpoint inhibitor, nivolumab, for 16 cycles of treatment while being followed up by an endocrinologist to monitor any side effects. A repeat CT/PET FDG 12 months later showed complete disappearance of the left lung nodule, indicating tumor remission (Figure ).
What is the patient's albumin corrected delta ratio? You should use the patient's medical values and health status when they were first admitted to the hospital prior to any treatment.

step_info.output:
Clinical Calculation: Albumin Corrected Delta Ratio

1. Required Clinical Variables:
   - Na: 140 mEq/L
   - Cl: 100 mEq/L
   - HCO3: 18 mEq/L
   - Albumin: 3.0 g/dL

2. Formulas Used:
   - Corrected Anion Gap = (Na - Cl - HCO3) + 2.5 * (4.0 - Albumin)
   - Albumin Corrected Delta Ratio = (Corrected Anion Gap - 12) / (24 - HCO3)

3. Calculation Steps:
   Step A: Calculate Corrected Anion Gap
   - Substitution: (140 - 100 - 18) + 2.5 * (4.0 - 3.0)
   - Arithmetic: (22) + 2.5 * (1.0)
   - Arithmetic: 22 + 2.5 = 24.5

   Step B: Calculate Albumin Corrected Delta Ratio
   - Substitution: (24.5 - 12) / (24 - 18)
   - Arithmetic: 12.5 / 6
   - Result: 2.0833

4. Final Result:
   2.08 (unitless ratio)

step_info.thought: The tool returned a calculation example, but I must now use the patient's actual labs from the note at the time of admission. The note states: "Na: 131 mEq/L, K: 4.1 mEq/L, Cl: 87 mEq/L, CO2: 27 mEq/L... albumin: 3.7 g/dL". I will now calculate the Albumin Corrected Delta Ratio using these specific values.

step_info.args: {"focus": "Calculate the Albumin Corrected Delta Ratio for a patient with Na=131, Cl=87, HCO3=27, and Albumin=3.7. Formula: Delta Ratio = (Anion Gap - 12) / (24 - HCO3). Corrected Anion Gap = (Na - (Cl + HCO3)) + 2.5 * (4.0 - Albumin)."}

step_info.output:
Clinical Calculation Summary: Albumin Corrected Delta Ratio

Extracted Variables:
- Serum Sodium (Na): 131 mEq/L
- Serum Chloride (Cl): 87 mEq/L
- Bicarbonate (HCO3): 27 mEq/L
- Serum Albumin: 3.7 g/dL

Formulas Applied:
1. Corrected Anion Gap = (Na - (Cl + HCO3)) + 2.5 * (4.0 - Albumin)
2. Delta Ratio = (Corrected Anion Gap - 12) / (24 - HCO3)

Step-by-Step Arithmetic:
1. Uncorrected Anion Gap: 131 - (87 + 27) = 131 - 114 = 17
2. Albumin Adjustment: 2.5 * (4.0 - 3.7) = 2.5 * 0.3 = 0.75
3. Corrected Anion Gap: 17 + 0.75 = 17.75
4. Delta Ratio Numerator: 17.75 - 12 = 5.75
5. Delta Ratio Denominator: 24 - 27 = -3
6. Delta Ratio: 5.75 / -3 = -1.92

Final Result:
- Albumin Corrected Anion Gap: 17.75
- Delta Ratio: -1.92

step_info.thought: I have performed the required calculation for the patient's Albumin Corrected Delta Ratio based on the laboratory values at the time of their hospital admission. The result of this calculation is -1.92.

step_info.output: Final result processed.

args:
A 65-year-old African American male with a history of chronic obstructive pulmonary disease (COPD), anemia, and heart failure with preserved ejection fraction (HFpEF) presented to the emergency department with complaints of unintentional weight loss, fevers, chills, and memory decline. He had lost 60 pounds within the past year, with associated functional decline and generalized fatigue. Within the past two months, he had developed fevers and night sweats, and within the past week, he had begun to experience cognitive decline described as being in a “fog,” which had led him to miss familiar exits on the highway and forget his way to his doctor’s office.
Upon presentation to the emergency department, his vitals included a blood pressure of 117/51 mmHg, heart rate of 95 beats per minute, respiratory rate of 18 breaths per minute, O2 saturation of 97%, and temperature of 98.3 °F. Physical exam showed a thin-appearing man in moderate distress secondary to shortness of breath and cognitive issues. Pertinent positives included bilateral shoddy cervical lymphadenopathy, increased respiratory effort with bilateral wheezing and inspiratory crackles, 1+ pretibial edema, and large bilateral axillary lymphadenopathy. A mini-mental status exam (MMSE) revealed a score of 24/30. Laboratory workup revealed WBC of 10.1 x 1000/mm3, Hemoglobin (Hb) of 9.5 g/dL, and platelet count of 400 x 1000/mm3. The basic metabolic panel (BMP) showed sodium of 130 mmol/L, potassium of 3.3 mEq/L, and creatinine of 0.84 mg/dL. Brain natriuretic peptide (BNP) was 225 pg/mL (normal range: 5-100). A chest X-ray showed cardiomegaly and mild interstitial edema with bilateral pleural effusions. CT brain revealed no acute findings. Given the patient’s functional decline, diffuse lymphadenopathy, and worsening mental status, he was admitted for further workup and management.
On day two of his admission, collateral information was obtained from the patient’s family, which revealed additional prior workup for his symptoms. Six months beforehand, the patient had been evaluated for worsening blurred vision and had been diagnosed with bilateral panuveitis. An extensive laboratory workup was performed, which is summarized below in Table .
Extensive radiology and pathology workup was performed and raised concerns for sarcoidosis. Although the patient had been prescribed methotrexate (MTX), he reported never starting it. One month prior to presentation, the patient had undergone a CT of the chest, abdomen, and pelvis with contrast, which had revealed multiple enlarged mediastinal and axillary lymph nodes and extensive bronchiectasis of bilateral lungs, with the lymphadenopathy (LAD) considered to be reactive versus neoplastic (Figure , Figure ).
Given this new information, rheumatology was consulted, inflammatory markers were drawn which showed an elevated C-reactive protein (CRP) of 50.4 mg/L (normal level: <5), and an erythrocyte sedimentation rate (ESR) of 97 mm/hr (normal range: 0-20); an excisional lymph node biopsy was pursued. Pathology from the axillary lymph node biopsy (Figure , Figure ) revealed increased epithelioid histiocytes and well-formed granulomas associated with multinucleated giant cells, most consistent with sarcoidosis. We also ruled out infectious etiologies with this biopsy with acid-fast bacilli (AFB) and Gomori methenamine silver (GMS) stains, both of which were negative, as shown in Figure and Figure . Given these results, initiating 40-mg prednisone and 15-mg MTX weekly was recommended. It is important to note that on every night during the patient’s hospitalization, he recorded a fever in the range of 101.4-102.4 °F. These fevers abated with acetaminophen and were never present during the day. Blood cultures were drawn at each instance, but they all returned free of organisms. The fevers were ultimately attributed to sarcoidosis.
Regarding the patient’s encephalopathy, neurology was consulted and an MRI brain was performed, which showed no evidence of infection, NS, CNS lymphoma, or neurodegenerative disease process. The patient’s mental status slowly improved over the course of the hospitalization, and a lumbar puncture was not pursued. In the outpatient setting, the patient was followed up with rheumatology and the steroid dose was slowly tapered. The patient also followed up with his primary care physician, stating improvements in his memory and his functional status. He has continued to live alone, retaining the ability to perform activities of daily living on his own.

What is the patient's free water deficit in terms of kg? The desired serum sodium concentration is 140 mEq/L. You should use the patient's medical values and health status when they were first admitted to the hospital prior to any treatment.

step_info.output:
Clinical Score Calculation: Free Water Deficit

1. Identified Clinical Variables:
   - Gender: Female
   - Weight: 70 kg
   - Serum Sodium (Na): 150 mEq/L

2. Formula Used:
   Free Water Deficit (Female) = 0.5 * Weight_kg * ((Na/140) - 1)

3. Substitution:
   0.5 * 70 * ((150 / 140) - 1)

4. Step-by-Step Arithmetic:
   - Sodium Ratio (150 / 140): 1.0714
   - Adjust Ratio (1.0714 - 1): 0.0714
   - Total Body Water Constant (0.5 * 70): 35
   - Final Product (35 * 0.0714): 2.499

5. Final Result:
   Free Water Deficit = 2.5 Liters

step_info.args: {"focus": "Extract data for Free Water Deficit calculation: weight (kg) and serum sodium (mEq/L) for a 65-year-old male from the patient note. Target Na is 140 mEq/L."}

step_info.output:
Clinical Calculation Summary: Free Water Deficit

--- Extracted Variables ---
- Patient Weight: 80 kg
- Biological Sex: Male
- Current Serum Sodium: 150 mEq/L
- Target Serum Sodium: 140 mEq/L

--- Formula ---
TBW (Total Body Water) = Weight (kg) * 0.6
Free Water Deficit = TBW * ((Current Na / Target Na) - 1)

--- Step-by-Step Arithmetic ---
1. Calculate TBW: 80 kg * 0.6 = 48.0 L
2. Calculate Sodium Ratio: 150 mEq/L / 140 mEq/L = 1.0714
3. Calculate Correction Factor: 1.0714 - 1 = 0.0714
4. Calculate Deficit: 48.0 L * 0.0714 = 3.4272 L

--- Final Result ---
Estimated Free Water Deficit: 3.43 Liters

step_info.args: {"focus": "Calculate Free Water Deficit for a male patient: weight = 80kg, Current Sodium = 130 mmol/L, Target Sodium = 140 mmol/L. Note: The patient note says sodium is 130. Use 80kg as estimated weight."}

step_info.output:
--- Clinical Calculation: Free Water Deficit ---

Extracted Variables:
- Patient Gender: Male
- Estimated Weight: 80 kg
- Current Sodium: 130 mmol/L
- Target Sodium: 140 mmol/L

Formula Applied:
Free Water Deficit = Total Body Water (TBW) * (1 - (Current Sodium / Target Sodium))
*Note: TBW calculated as 60% of body weight for adult males.

Step-by-Step Arithmetic:
1. Determine TBW: 80 kg * 0.6 = 48.0 L
2. Calculate Sodium Ratio: 130 / 140 = 0.9286
3. Calculate Deficit factor: 1 - 0.9286 = 0.0714
4. Compute final deficit: 48.0 L * 0.0714 = 3.4272 L

Final Result:
The calculated Free Water Deficit is 3.43 L.

step_info.args: {"focus": "The patient is hyponatremic (Na 130 mmol/L). The formula for free water deficit is usually for hypernatremia (Na > 140). Please confirm the math for free water deficit when current Na (130) is lower than target Na (140), or explain if the result is 0 because there is no free water deficit."}

step_info.output:
Calculation Summary: Free Water Deficit Analysis
--------------------------------------------------
Extracted Variables:
- Current Serum Sodium: 130 mmol/L
- Target Serum Sodium: 140 mmol/L
- Total Body Water (TBW): Not specified (requires patient weight/gender)

Formula Applied:
- Free Water Deficit = TBW * ((Current_Na / Target_Na) - 1)

Step-by-Step Arithmetic:
1. Calculate ratio: 130 mmol/L / 140 mmol/L ≈ 0.9286
2. Apply formula factor: 0.9286 - 1 = -0.0714
3. Clinical Interpretation: Because the Current Na (130) is lower than the Target Na (140), the formula yields a negative value.

Final Result:
0 Liters.
Conclusion: There is no free water deficit. The patient is hyponatremic; this formula is intended for hypernatremia and is clinically non-applicable in this context.
Final result processed.

args: A 74-year-old woman arrives in the pre-operative holding area for a planned right knee arthroscopy to address a symptomatic medial meniscal flap tear that has failed three months of structured physiotherapy and two corticosteroid injections; the orthopedic team anticipates fewer than forty minutes of scope time under general anesthesia with discharge later today if she meets ambulation and pain-control goals. She walked from the parking garage using a single-point cane only for confidence on stairs, reports that she still prepares her own meals, gardens in short bursts, and completes household chores without assistance, and nursing intake documents an independent transfer from wheelchair to stretcher without breathlessness or fatigue, confirming that her baseline activity level remains unrestricted for age. The forthcoming scope will be her first operative intervention in more than eight years, and the chart contains no record of any endoscopic or interventional radiology procedure during the past several weeks. Two weeks ago, however, she presented to an outside emergency department with gradually increasing ankle edema, orthopnea, and a 4-kg weight gain over ten days; chest radiography then showed pulmonary vascular congestion and BNP was 960 pg mL⁻¹. Intravenous furosemide was started through a single-lumen peripherally inserted central catheter placed in the left basilic vein; she received three days of gentle diuresis on a telemetry floor, lost the excess fluid, and was discharged on her chronic regimen of torsemide 20 mg and spironolactone 25 mg daily. The PICC remains in place beneath a clear occlusive dressing, flushes without resistance, and returns dark venous blood this morning, and there is no erythema, induration, or tenderness along the tract. She denies fever, chills, night sweats, productive cough, or sputum change since that admission, and there has been no recent antibiotic therapy. Past medical history also includes moderate chronic obstructive pulmonary disease attributed to a 50-pack-year cigarette exposure; she quit twelve years ago, utilizes tiotropium inhaled once daily, and keeps a short-acting beta-agonist for exertional dyspnea but has not required oral steroids or hospitalization for an exacerbation in the last year. In addition, she carries a long-standing diagnosis of pancolonic ulcerative colitis that is in clinical and endoscopic remission on mesalamine 2.4 g daily, with her last colonoscopy nine months earlier showing only mild scattered pseudopolyps and no dysplasia. She has never been told of clots in her legs or lungs, recalls no unilateral calf swelling, pleuritic chest pain, or episodes of unexplained tachycardia, and duplex ultrasonography obtained three years ago for nocturnal leg cramps demonstrated fully compressible deep venous segments bilaterally. No first-degree relatives have suffered venous thromboembolism, and she has never been advised of any inherited clotting tendency. She denies prior myocardial infarction, stroke, spinal cord injury, hip or pelvic fractures, or periods of enforced bed rest beyond the brief hospital stay for heart failure noted above. She has never worn long-term casts or braces, and she resumed her usual daily walking circuit around the neighborhood within five days of that discharge. Medications reviewed at bedside include torsemide, spironolactone, carvedilol 12.5 mg twice daily, lisinopril 10 mg once daily, tiotropium inhaled 18 µg, albuterol MDI as needed, mesalamine, calcium-vitamin D supplementation, and acetaminophen 500 mg every six hours for knee discomfort. She takes no systemic corticosteroids, hormone replacement, selective estrogen-receptor modulators, erythropoiesis-stimulating agents, or antiplatelet therapy apart from a baby aspirin that was held last night on anesthesia’s recommendation. She drinks half a glass of red wine with dinner on weekends, denies illicit drug use, and her weight this morning is 68 kg at a height of 157 cm, yielding a body-mass index of 27 kg m⁻². Vital signs ten minutes before review record temperature 36.6 °C, blood pressure 118/66 mm Hg, heart rate 72 beats min⁻¹ in regular rhythm, respiratory rate 14 min⁻¹, and oxygen saturation 97 % breathing ambient air. Airway assessment reveals a Mallampati class II view, full cervical flexion-extension, and intact dentition. Jugular venous contour is not elevated at 30° inclination, carotid upstrokes are brisk, and the point of maximal impulse is nondisplaced. Cardiac auscultation identifies a soft S₃ but no murmurs, clicks, or pericardial rubs; there is trace pedal edema only on dependent positioning with prompt resolution on elevation. Lung fields are clear to auscultation without wheezes or crackles, and expiratory phase is mildly prolonged yet symmetrical. Abdominal examination shows a soft, non-tender belly with normal bowel sounds and no organomegaly. Inspection of both lower extremities reveals well-hydrated skin, no varicosities, no pitting edema at the mid-shin, and calf circumferences measured 10 cm below the tibial tuberosities are 34 cm bilaterally and equal; Homan’s sign is not elicited. Distal pulses are palpable at 2+ in dorsalis pedis and posterior tibial arteries. Neurologic screen demonstrates full orientation, normal speech, and 5/5 strength in all major muscle groups; tandem gait is steady when she walks six steps unassisted. The only indwelling vascular device is the previously noted PICC; a separate 20-gauge peripheral IV in the right cephalic vein is running lactated Ringer’s at keep-open rate for anesthetic induction, and no additional lines, drains, or urinary catheters are present. Morning laboratory results drawn at 06:15 through the PICC reveal hemoglobin 12.8 g dL⁻¹, hematocrit 38 %, platelets 241 × 10⁹ L⁻¹, leukocytes 6.4 × 10⁹ L⁻¹, sodium 138 mmol L⁻¹, potassium 4.3 mmol L⁻¹, bicarbonate 25 mmol L⁻¹, creatinine 0.96 mg dL⁻¹, AST 20 U L⁻¹, ALT 18 U L⁻¹, total bilirubin 0.7 mg dL⁻¹, prothrombin time 12.2 s with an international normalized ratio of 1.0, and activated partial thromboplastin time 30 s. A 12-lead electrocardiogram shows normal sinus rhythm at 70 beats min⁻¹ with left ventricular hypertrophy by voltage criteria but no ischemic changes. Posterior-anterior chest radiograph obtained yesterday for pre-operative clearance depicts mild cardiomegaly, clear lung fields, and the PICC tip residing at the cavo-atrial junction. Sequential pneumatic compression sleeves are already applied to both calves but remain uninflated pending draping, and chemoprophylaxis has been withheld until after skin closure given the short duration of the case. She has been nil per os since midnight, voided at 07:00 without dysuria, removed her hearing aids and dentures, and articulates understanding of the surgical plan, anesthetic risks, and the importance of early postoperative ambulation and incentive spirometry.
